vectors.label: Vectors: meaning, dimension, and basic operations
vectors.summary: Start with what a vector really is, how dimension works, and how to add or scale vectors.

numpy.label: NumPy shapes: 1D, row, column, broadcasting
numpy.summary: Learn the difference between mathematical dimension and NumPy shape so your code behaves the way you expect.

dot.label: Dot product: one number that measures alignment
dot.summary: Compute dot products and connect the sign of the result to geometry and angle.

products.label: Hadamard vs outer product
products.summary: Separate element-wise multiplication from matrix-building multiplication.

projection.label: Projection: the part that points along a direction
projection.summary: Use projection to isolate the along-the-line part of a vector and separate it from the perpendicular part.

basics.title: Vectors: packets of numbers with meaning
basics.bodyMarkdown:
A vector is an **ordered list of numbers** treated as one object.

Think of a delivery drone:
- one number tells how far east or west it moved
- one number tells how far north or south it moved
- maybe another number tells how far up or down it moved

That bundle of numbers is a **vector**.

---

## Two useful ways to think about a vector

### 1) Data view
A vector can store information like:
- monthly sales
- temperatures across cities
- RGB color values

### 2) Geometry view
A vector can also be an **arrow**:
- it has a **direction**
- it has a **length**

---

## Dimension vs orientation

If a vector has 3 entries, it lives in $\mathbb{R}^3$.
That is its **dimension**.

But it may be **written** as either:
- a **column vector**
- a **row vector**

Those are different **layouts**, not different mathematical dimensions.

---

## Operations you should know first

### Add vectors
Add entry by entry.

### Scale a vector
Multiply every entry by the same number.

That is why vectors are so useful: they let you move, measure, and transform information in a structured way.

numpy.title: NumPy shapes: where coding meets linear algebra
numpy.bodyMarkdown:
In math, a vector is mostly about the **entries**.
In NumPy, a vector is also about **shape**.

That difference causes a lot of beginner confusion.

---

## Same numbers, different shapes

~~~python
np.array([1,2,3])        # shape (3,)
np.array([[1,2,3]])      # shape (1,3)
np.array([[1],[2],[3]])  # shape (3,1)
~~~

All of these contain the same values, but NumPy stores them differently.

---

## Why this matters

When shapes differ, operations can behave very differently.

For example, broadcasting can turn what looks like “vector addition” into a whole matrix.

So in code, always ask:
- how many entries are there?
- what is the array shape?

Those are not the same question.

dot.title: Dot product: measuring alignment
dot.bodyMarkdown:
The dot product takes **two vectors** and returns **one number**.

That number tells you how much the two vectors point in the same direction.

---

## Intuition

Imagine pushing a box across the floor.
If your force points mostly in the same direction as the motion, the effect is strong.
If your force points sideways, it contributes very little.

The dot product captures that idea of **alignment**.

---

## Sign meaning

- positive dot product → mostly same direction
- zero dot product → perpendicular
- negative dot product → mostly opposite direction

---

## Algebra formula

$$
a\cdot b = a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + \cdots + a_nb_n
$$

So the dot product is both:
- a concrete computation
- a geometric meaning

products.title: Hadamard vs outer product
products.bodyMarkdown:
Students often hear “multiply vectors” and assume there is only one meaning.
There isn’t.

---

## Hadamard product

This is **entry-by-entry multiplication**.

It only works when both vectors have the same number of entries.

---

## Outer product

This takes one vector and multiplies it against another to build a **matrix**.

So instead of returning a vector or a single number, it returns a rectangular grid of values.

---

## Why the distinction matters

These operations answer different questions:
- Hadamard: compare entries pair by pair
- Outer product: build all pairwise products into a matrix

Same word “multiply,” very different result.

projection.title: Projection: the shadow along a direction
projection.bodyMarkdown:
Projection answers this question:

> How much of one vector points along another direction?

A good mental image is a flashlight.
A vector casts a kind of **shadow** onto a line.
That shadow is the **projection**.

---

## Why this matters

Projection lets you split a vector into two parts:
- the part that lies **along** a chosen direction
- the part that is **perpendicular** to it

That idea shows up everywhere:
- physics
- graphics
- data science
- least squares

---

## Core formula

$$
\operatorname{proj}_a(b)=\frac{a\cdot b}{a\cdot a}a
$$

The scalar in front tells you **how far along** vector $a$ you should go.

la_vectors_dimension.title: Vector dimension
la_vectors_dimension.hint: Count the number of entries. That tells you the vector space.

options.a: It belongs to the vector space matching its number of entries
options.b: It belongs to a space with one fewer entry
options.c: It belongs to a space with one extra entry

la_vectors_orientation.title: Row or column?
la_vectors_orientation.hint: A row goes across. A column goes up and down.

la_vectors_membership.title: Membership in R^n
la_vectors_membership.hint: If there are n entries, the vector lives in R^n.

la_vectors_scalar_mult_input.title: Scale the vector
la_vectors_scalar_mult_input.hint: Multiply every component by the scalar.

la_numpy_shapes_basic.title: Basic NumPy shapes
la_numpy_shapes_basic.prompt: Which set of shapes is correct for `asArray`, `rowVec`, and `colVec`?
la_numpy_shapes_basic.hint: `np.array([1,2,3])` is `(3,)`, a row is `(1,3)`, and a column is `(3,1)`.

la_numpy_column_shape.title: Column vector shape
la_numpy_column_shape.prompt: What is the NumPy shape of `np.array([[1],[2],[3]])`?

la_numpy_broadcast_shape.title: Broadcasting result
la_numpy_broadcast_shape.prompt: If `v` has shape `(3,1)` and `w` has shape `(1,2)`, what shape does `v + w` have?

la_numpy_list_vs_array.title: List vs array multiplication
la_numpy_list_vs_array.prompt: What is true about `a * 2` for a Python list and a NumPy array?
la_numpy_list_vs_array.hint: A Python list repeats. A NumPy array scales entry by entry.

la_dot_sign_angle.title: Dot sign and angle
la_dot_sign_angle.hint: Positive means acute, zero means right, negative means obtuse.

la_dot_zero_means.title: What does zero dot product mean?
la_dot_zero_means.prompt: If `a · b = 0`, what is the usual geometric interpretation?
la_dot_zero_means.hint: Zero dot product usually signals orthogonality.

la_dot_self_norm.title: Dot product with itself
la_dot_self_norm.hint: A vector dotted with itself gives the squared norm.

la_products_outer_shape.title: Outer product shape
la_products_outer_shape.hint: If a has m entries and b has n entries, `ab^T` is `m × n`.

la_products_outer_entry.title: One entry of the outer product
la_products_outer_entry.hint: Multiply the chosen entry from the left vector by the chosen entry from the right vector.

la_products_hadamard_vs_dot.title: Hadamard vs dot
la_products_hadamard_vs_dot.hint: Hadamard returns entry-wise products. Dot returns one number.

options.a: Both always return a single number
options.b: Hadamard returns a vector-like array; dot returns one number
options.c: Hadamard and dot are the same
options.d: Outer product and dot are the same

la_products_hadamard_meaning.title: Meaning of Hadamard product
la_products_hadamard_meaning.prompt: What does the Hadamard product do?

options.b: It makes a matrix from every pair

la_projection_meaning.title: Meaning of projection
la_projection_meaning.prompt: What does projecting `b` onto `a` tell you?
la_projection_meaning.hint: Think of the shadow of `b` along the direction of `a`.

la_projection_formula.title: Projection formula
la_projection_formula.prompt: Which formula gives the projection of `b` onto `a`?

la_projection_perp_zero.title: Projection of a perpendicular vector
la_projection_perp_zero.prompt: If `b` is perpendicular to `a`, what is the projection of `b` onto `a`?
la_projection_perp_zero.hint: Perpendicular means the dot product is zero.

la_projection_split.title: Orthogonal decomposition
la_projection_split.prompt: A vector can be split into which two parts relative to a direction?
la_projection_split.hint: One part lies along the direction and the other is perpendicular.
